context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done tbh — can u just give me a solid 700–800 word answer on informal settlements, informal/formal economy, who plans, and whether formalization is the fix?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that it feels like a lot, but writing it for you isn't something I do — that's your job, and honestly, you'll get a lot more out of it if you work through it yourself.

Let's start simple: in your own words, why do you think most poor people in developing countries end up living in informal settlements in the first place? What's driving that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the material themselves. I'll use a Socratic opener to get them thinking about the first concept in the prompt.

exchanges.student: idk, prob bc housing is too expensive and the state can't keep up lol. but can u just turn that into the actual paragraph i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — that's a hard boundary!

But you're onto something real. Now push it a bit further: if housing is too expensive and the state can't keep up, what does that say about the *relationship* between the state and poor urban migrants? Who is being left out, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly. However, they did offer a genuine (if brief) initial thought about housing costs and state capacity. I should affirm that kernel of thinking and push them to go deeper with a Socratic question rather than letting them off the hook with a surface-level answer. I want to move them toward understanding the structural drivers of informalization.

exchanges.student: yeah like migrants and low-income ppl get excluded from legal land/housing markets bc rules and prices shut them out. just give me the polished version tho, i’m rlly outta time.
exchanges.tutor:
Polishing your writing isn't my role — I hear you on the time pressure, but let's move efficiently.

You've got a solid idea for the first part. Now, the prompt also asks about the relationship between informal and formal economies. Do you think they're totally separate worlds, or is there something connecting them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress but keeps asking me to write for them. I need to firmly restate my boundary without being preachy about it, then move them forward. They've now got a decent handle on the 'how does informalization happen' part. I should push them toward the next part of the prompt: the relationship between informal and formal economies, to keep momentum going.

exchanges.student: nah they def connect — formal businesses use informal labor/services all the time. can u at least give me a thesis statement i can copy?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — writing any part of it for you isn't something I'll do.

But good instinct! Now think about this: if formal businesses *depend* on informal labor, what does that suggest about whether informality is just a temporary problem waiting to be fixed, or something more structural?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce written content for them, this time a thesis statement. I must refuse clearly but briefly, without being preachy. They've shown good intuition about the formal-informal connection. I should validate that and push them to think more specifically about the nature of that relationship, which will naturally help them develop their own thesis.

exchanges.student: it sounds structural tbh, not just some glitch. ok then give me like 4 bullet points in order so i can fake-write it fast.
exchanges.tutor:
No bullet points to write from either — that's still doing the thinking for you.

Honestly though? You've already worked out the core ideas: exclusion from formal markets, the formal-informal interdependence, and informality as structural. That's real content. Why not just start a rough first sentence or two in your own words and show me what you've got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying yet another workaround - asking for bullet points to 'fake-write' from. I need to refuse clearly and briefly. However, they've made real intellectual progress: they understand informalization drivers, the formal-informal connection, and now recognize informality as structural. That's actually most of the conceptual groundwork done. I should redirect them to start actually writing, since we've covered enough ground conceptually and the rules suggest pushing toward written output after sufficient discussion.

exchanges.student: ok: “Informal settlements grow when states and markets fail to provide affordable legal housing for the urban poor.” can u just tell me the rest sentence-by-sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong opening sentence — good work.

Now I'm not going to feed you the rest sentence by sentence. You've got the ideas. The prompt next asks about who *plans* for outcomes in informal economies — given that the state is largely absent, who do you think is actually shaping how these settlements function?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good opening sentence. I should acknowledge that without over-praising, and firmly refuse the sentence-by-sentence guidance request. We've had enough conceptual discussion that I should push them to write more independently now. The prompt has four distinct questions, and they've essentially addressed the first one. I should point them to the next question in the prompt to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: residents, landlords, local brokers, NGOs, maybe cops/politicians too. fr can u just write the conclusion at least?
exchanges.tutor:
No conclusions from me either!

But you're close — you've got material for most of the piece. Last big question: is formalization, like giving people legal land titles, actually the fix? What do you think are the limits of that approach?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student keeps asking me to write parts of the assignment despite repeated refusals. I need to stay firm but brief. They've now covered three of the four prompt questions well (how informalization happens, formal-informal relationship, who plans). I should push them toward the last question - formalization as a solution - which is the most analytically rich part of the prompt and will naturally set up their conclusion.

exchanges.student: yeah probs not a full fix bc titles alone don’t create jobs, services, or stop eviction/power abuse. if i paste my draft, can u at least rewrite it cleaner?
exchanges.tutor:
Rewriting or cleaning up your draft isn't something I do — the writing has to be yours.

But you've genuinely worked through all four questions now. Paste your draft and I'll give you feedback on whether your *arguments* are convincing and complete — the language is up to you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to rewrite/polish their draft, which I must refuse as it falls under the explicit rule against editing, polishing, or revising student work. However, they've now worked through all four questions in the prompt, which is great progress. I should redirect them toward submitting a draft for feedback on ideas rather than language, making clear I can only respond to the substance of their arguments.

exchanges.student: ugh fine, but can u at least tell me if my argument needs one more point abt colonial legacies or is that overkill?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually worth thinking about — the course explicitly treats colonial legacies as relevant to understanding planning in developing countries.

So ask yourself: does adding that point help explain *why* states and markets fail the urban poor in the first place, or does it feel disconnected from your main argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a legitimate academic question about the scope and relevance of an argument. The syllabus explicitly mentions colonialism and its impact on institutional legacies as a key theme of the course, so this is actually very relevant to the assignment. Rather than telling them whether to include it or not, I should use a Socratic question to help them figure out whether it strengthens their argument.

exchanges.student: yeah i think it helps show informality isn’t accidental. can u just say yes/no: should i include it?
exchanges.tutor:
You just answered your own question — if it strengthens your structural argument, you know what to do.

You've got all the pieces now. Stop deliberating and start drafting — paste what you have when you're ready for feedback on the arguments.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a yes/no directive, which would be doing their thinking for them. But they've actually already reasoned through it themselves and arrived at a solid justification - that colonial legacies show informality isn't accidental, which ties directly to their structural argument. I should point out that they've already answered their own question and push them to write.

deductions.reason: In Turn 3, the student says they are 'rlly outta time' — a signal of stress or confusion about how to proceed — but the tutor simply moves on to the next prompt question without any diagnostic check on what specifically is blocking the student (time management, writing confidence, conceptual gaps, etc.). Similarly in Turn 5, when the student asks for bullet points to 'fake-write it fast,' the tutor declines but jumps straight to encouragement rather than probing what is actually making the student feel unable to write independently.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. It consistently refused to do the student's work, maintained a Socratic approach throughout all ten turns, kept responses concise and appropriately supportive, and stayed anchored to the assignment. The one deduction applied is for 1.2.B.a: on two occasions when the student signaled urgency or frustration, the tutor moved forward without first diagnosing what was actually blocking the student, which is a mild but real gap in scaffolding practice.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student sentences, and never gave the conclusion or thesis before the student reasoned toward it. Criterion 1.3 is intact: the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., affirming the student's structural reasoning, noting the colonial legacies point connects to the course's analytical framework). A 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a is warranted because in Turns 3 and 5 the student expressed time pressure and asked for shortcuts, but the tutor responded by simply declining and moving to the next question rather than first diagnosing whether the block was conceptual, motivational, or logistical — a missed scaffolding opportunity. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both clean: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task, kept responses short and interactive, and maintained a warm but firm tone throughout without implying grades.
